serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Dezhou Braised Chicken is approximately 200 g of edible meat, representing roughly one-quarter of a whole bird, commonly consumed as a main dish at lunch or dinner.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, broilers or fryers, meat only, cooked, roasted — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No approved source contains a direct entry for Dezhou Braised Chicken. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked chicken (roasted, meat only) was used as the primary nutritional anchor. Values for energy, protein, fat, and carbohydrate were cross-referenced against the input AI-generated estimates (provenance: enriched_json, confidence 0.9). The input values are broadly consistent with USDA cooked chicken data adjusted upward for fat and sodium due to deep-frying and high-sodium master stock braising. Sodium (450 mg/100 g) reflects the braising stock and is plausible for this preparation. Saturated fat estimated at 3.8 g/100 g based on USDA fatty acid profile for cooked chicken (approximately 26% of total fat). Micronutrient values retained from input where consistent with USDA cooked chicken ranges. All values documented as adapted estimates.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care, American Diabetes Association (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dezhou Braised Chicken. GI estimated at approximately 40 (low) based on the analogous category of cooked chicken with minor carbohydrate additions (honey/maltose glaze). The University of Sydney GI Database classifies plain cooked chicken and similar high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dishes as low GI (GI < 55). The overall carbohydrate content of 2.5 g/100 g is very low; the high protein (18.5 g) and fat (14.5 g) content further attenuate any glycemic response. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (40 × 5.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.0, which is negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (450 mg/100 g) from master stock braising
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to no more than 100 g per meal, as a 200 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg — and should request reduced-sodium preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (2.5 g/100 g) with high protein and fat moderating glycemic response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dezhou Braised Chicken is suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions; the low carbohydrate load (estimated GL ≈ 2 per 200 g serving) poses minimal glycemic risk, though the honey/maltose glaze contributes a small amount of sugar and the high sodium content warrants monitoring for individuals with concurrent hypertension.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from deep-frying and master stock
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: People with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in limited portions (≤100 g) due to the high sodium content (450 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (≈3.8 g/100 g) contributed by the deep-frying step; choosing lean breast meat portions and removing skin can reduce saturated fat intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory spices balanced against pro-inflammatory saturated fat from deep-frying
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The spices in the master stock (ginger, cinnamon, star anise) have documented anti-inflammatory properties; however, the deep-frying step adds saturated fat which may promote systemic inflammation, so individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should prefer smaller portions and skin-free servings.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dezhou Braised Chicken (德州扒鸡, Dézhōu pájī), also known as Five-Spice Deboned Braised Chicken (五香脱骨扒鸡), is a celebrated traditional dish originating from Dezhou city in Shandong Province, northern China, with a culinary history exceeding 300 years. It is widely regarded as the 'Number One Chicken in China' and is a protected regional specialty. The preparation is distinctive: a whole chicken is first coated with honey or maltose syrup and deep-fried until the skin turns a deep mahogany-red, then slowly braised for several hours in an aged 'master stock' (老汤, lǎotāng) containing more than 20 spices and medicinal herbs including star anise, cloves, cinnamon, Sichuan pepper, ginger, and fennel. The prolonged braising renders the meat exceptionally tender — so soft that the flesh separates cleanly from the bone with minimal effort — while the skin remains intact and lacquered. The flavour profile is deeply savoury, mildly sweet from the glaze, and richly aromatic from the spice blend. Dezhou Braised Chicken is traditionally served whole or halved, either warm or at room temperature, and is a popular travel food and gift item due to its shelf stability when cold. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides approximately 215 kcal, 18.5 g protein, 14.5 g fat, and only 2.5 g carbohydrate, making it a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish. Sodium content is elevated at 450 mg/100 g due to the master stock, which is a key clinical consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated as low (approximately 40), and the glycemic load per standard 200 g serving is negligible at approximately 2.0, making it appropriate for people with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions.

confidence_reason: No approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia, FAO/INFOODS SEA) contains a direct entry for Dezhou Braised Chicken. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cooked chicken data and cross-referenced against the AI-generated input estimates (confidence 0.9). The adaptation is well-reasoned and internally consistent, but the absence of an exact approved-source match limits confidence to medium. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database principles for high-protein, low-carbohydrate cooked meat dishes.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE ADAPTATION: No approved source contains a direct entry for Dezhou Braised Chicken. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (cooked chicken, roasted, meat only) was used as the primary anchor. Input AI-generated values were retained where consistent with USDA ranges. Saturated fat (3.8 g/100 g) estimated from USDA fatty acid profile for cooked chicken (~26% of total fat). Cholesterol (85 mg/100 g) estimated from USDA cooked chicken data. Monounsaturated fat (5.9 g) and polyunsaturated fat (3.1 g) estimated from USDA cooked chicken fatty acid proportions. Trans fat set to 0.0 g as negligible for this preparation. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: water_g is set to -1 as detailed moisture data for this specific braised-and-fried preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source consulted as of 2026-04-17. 3. GI ESTIMATION: GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Dezhou Braised Chicken exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of the access date. 4. HALAL STATUS: Not confirmed as halal; master stock composition and slaughter method are not specified in available sources. 5. PORTIONS: Input contained a duplicate portion entry (two identical '1 serving 200g' records); deduplicated to a single default serving of 200 g. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are approximate matches; no exact ontology entry exists for this specific regional preparation. 7.
